coredata.dc:title: Matrix-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots with room temperature phosphorescence for information encryption and temperature detection
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Yujing
Chen, Xuwei
Wang, Yuqin
Lai, Wenqiang
Zheng, Nan
Weng, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Matrix-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots (n-CDs) with room temperature phosphorescence were successfully prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared n-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and water solubility and show blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. More importantly, the n-CDs exhibit a stable phosphorescence and their phosphorescence lifetime is 139 ms. Owing to vibration, the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder linearly decreases with increasing temperature. The as-prepared n-CD test paper can be applied in temperature monitoring as a probe; the monitoring results are consistent with those of a traditional thermometer. Furthermore, the n-CD solution can also be used in the field of advanced information security as an encryption ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Temperature detection
Security ink
Information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (Nos. 2018H0030), the Education Bureau of Fujian Province of China (Nos. JZ160453), and the Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training of Students Platform (Nos. S202110402066). The funder had no role in study design, in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, in the writing of the report, and nor in the decision to submit the article for publication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of n-CDs. (b) HRTEM images of n-CDs. (c) Size distribution of n-CDs. (d) Viability of HeLa cells after 48 h treatment with different concentrations of n-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of n-CDs. (f) XPS full spectra of n-CDs. (g) High-resolution XPS C 1 s spectra of n-CDs. (h) High-resolution XPS N 1 s spectra of n-CDs. (i) High-resolution XPS O 1 s spectra of n-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorbance (red line), PLE and PL spectra of n-CD powder. (Inset: n-CD powder under daylight and UV light (365 nm) irradiation). (b) Phosphorescence emission spectra of n-CD powder. (c) RTP decay spectra of n-CD powder (emission wavelength of 528 nm under excitation at 365 nm).

(a) Phosphorescence intensities of n-CD powder during continuous excitation with a UV beam (λex = 365 nm). Irradiation time: 0 to 60 min. (b) Phosphorescence emission spectra of n-CD powder in air and N2 (λex = 365 nm). (c) Phosphorescence spectra of n-CD powder at different temperatures from 20 to 70 °C (λex = 365 nm). (d) Phosphorescent digital photographs of n-CD-based test paper at different temperatures. (e) Temperature dependence of phosphorescence intensities of n-CD-based test paper.

(a) Images of RTP n-CD-based ink on filter paper under daylight and 365 nm UV light irradiation (on and off). (b) Images of prints with and without RTP n-CD-based ink on filter paper under daylight and 365 nm UV light irradiation (on and off).

head.ce:title: Matrix-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots with room temperature phosphorescence for information encryption and temperature detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Matrix-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots (n-CDs) with room temperature phosphorescence were successfully prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared n-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and water solubility and show blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. More importantly, the n-CDs exhibit a stable phosphorescence and their phosphorescence lifetime is 139 ms. Owing to vibration, the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder linearly decreases with increasing temperature. The as-prepared n-CD test paper can be applied in temperature monitoring as a probe; the monitoring results are consistent with those of a traditional thermometer. Furthermore, the n-CD solution can also be used in the field of advanced information security as an encryption ink.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical materials with long lifetimes, especially those with delayed luminescence visualization, have significant applications in several fields such as photocatalysis, photoelectricity, security, and imaging [1–5]. In recent years, phosphorescence is gaining increased attention because it is one of the most interesting sub-types of delayed luminescence. However, most phosphorescent materials can achieve phosphorescence only at ultra-low temperatures because phosphorescence is hampered by the spin-forbidden nature of three-state exciton transition and non-radiative decay[6]. To solve this problem, many methods have been used to achieve room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [7–11]. In the 1930 s, Clapp observed a brilliant green-to-blue afterglow from solid crystalline tetraphenylmethane and tetraphenylsilane derivatives with a visible duration up to 23 s at room temperature [12]. Kropp and Dawson reported that coronene and deuterated coronene exhibit long-lived phosphorescence at room temperature with lifetimes of 6 and 23 s in 1967 [13]. In the 1970 s, Bilen et al. reported room temperature emissions with lifetimes up to 4.85 s from solid crystals of carbazole, dibenzothiophene, dibenzofuran, and triphenylene [14]. In 2007, Fraser et al. reported an unusual room temperature phosphorescence that can last for 5–10 s with lifetime of 0.17 s [15]. Then, in 2013, Hirata et al. developed organic host–guest materials with efficient red–greenblue afterglow with a lifetime > 1 s in air at room temperature [16]. In recent years, people are based on the following two principles: enhancing spin–orbit coupling and restricting vibration and rotation [17–19]. Organometallic complexes (metal-containing) and pure organic compounds (metal-free) have been extensively studied [20,21]. However, these RTP materials have disadvantages such as a high cost, high toxicity, and complex preparation [22–26]. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare room-temperature phosphorescent materials that are non-toxic, low-cost, and environmentally friendly using low-cost raw materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained increased attention as novel materials. They possess several advantages, such as a simple preparation, tunable optical properties, good bio-compatibility, and strong fluorescence stability [27–31]. Therefore, CDs have been widely applied in sensing, optoelectronics, catalysis, and bioimaging [32–38]. It is worth noting that RTP CDs were discovered by embedding them in various matrices (such as PVA, polyurethane, urea/biuret, boric acid, and zeolites) to stabilize their excited triplet state [39–43]. In recent years, there have been a few reports on matrix-free RTP CDs [44,45]. In 2018, Wei Feng et al. report solid state fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots first show green self-protective RTP on filter paper without any treatment [46]. In the same year, Bai Yang’s group reporta feasible and facile route to produce metal-free RTP materials by directly constructing Polymer carbon dots through hydrothermal synthesis without the need for additional matrices compositing [19]. Two years later, Ching-Ping Wong shows a facile and effective strategy to prepare water-soluble boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) dots RTP materials without matrix protection. The as-prepared BCNO dots exhibit unexpectedly green afterglow with an ultralong lifetime of 1.57 s [47]. A one-step method for preparing matrix-free RTP CDs is the current focus of research.

In this study, we use urea, tartaric acid, and acrylamide to synthesize matrix-free RTP nitrogen-doped CDs (n-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method. Acrylamide can be polymerized to form polyacrylamide under a high temperature. The formed polyacrylamide can provide several H-bonds and stabilize the excited triplet species. At the same time, the doped nitrogen element can enhance the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) of n-CDs. For the above reasons, the as-prepared matrix-free RTP n-CDs possess stable phosphorescence properties, and their phosphorescence lifetime is 139 ms. The n-CD aqueous solution as an encryption ink can also be applied in information encryption and decryption because of its RTP properties and good water solubility. In addition, because vibration quenches the long-lived triplet excitons, the phosphorescence intensity and the n-CD powder lifetime is affected by temperature. Thus, the n-CD powder can be used as a thermometer for temperature detection. This study provides a novel method to synthesize matrix-free RTP CDs and demonstrates the high potential of CDs in developing new optical materials with unique properties.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Beijing Chemical Reagent. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ·cm−1) was used for all experiments. All solid samples were dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h before sample characterization.

The morphology and size of the n-CDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI/Philips Tecnai G2F20 TWIN TEM) and a dynamic light scattering instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS, ZEN 3690, Malvern). The KRATOS Axis ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Bruker Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Hyperion) were used to study the composition and surface groups of the n-CDs. Perkin Elmer UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750) and Horiba Jobin Yvon luminescence spectrometer (Fluoro Max-4) were used to characterize the fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of the n-CDs.

Urea (4 g), tartaric acid (1 g), acrylamide (4 g), and ultrapure water (15 mL) were added to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was heated in an electro-thermostatic blast oven at 200 °C for 36 h. After cooling to 25 °C, to remove impurities, the solution was filled into a dialysis bag (500 Da) and dialyzed with ultrapure water. The light brown n-CD solution was freeze-dried, and n-CD powder was obtained.

n-CDs-1 with no RTP was synthesized according to a previous study [31]. 0.5 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea under the same reaction conditions. The n-CDs-1 solution was obtained and processed in the same way as the n-CD solution.

Typical formulation: 150 mg n-CDs and 150 mg n-CDs-1 powder were added to 30 mL ultrapure water for preparing the n-CD-based ink and n-CDs-1-based ink, respectively. They can be directly used as encryption inks for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. The two types of inks were injected into an empty commercial inkjet printer cartridge (EPSON L 310 printer).

The HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24 h at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Different concentrations of n-CDs were added into 96-well plates containing these cells. After 48 h incubation, the relative viability of the cell samples was determined using MTT assay methods to assess the metabolic activity of the cells treated with n-CDs. The cells were lysed using acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate. Absorbance was recorded at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

Matrix-free RTP n-CDs were successfully synthesized using urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide by a one-step hydrothermal method. The preparation steps are shown in Fig. 1 . First, the morphology, structure, and composition of the n-CDs were studied using TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, the TEM image and size distribution indicate that the n-CDs have a good dispersion and their size ranges from 3.5 nm to 6.5 nm (average size: ∼5 nm). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the n-CDs has a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is in accordance with the (100) facets of graphite (Fig. 2b) [48]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the cell viability remains at ∼ 90% when the concentration of the n-CDs is 800 μg·mL−1. This result indicates that the as-prepared n-CDs had no obvious cytotoxicity. Fig. 1e shows the FT-IR spectra of the n-CDs. The characteristic peaks at ∼ 3429 and ∼ 3282 cm−1 resulted from the –OH and –NH2 stretching vibrations. The –CH2– stretching vibrations were located at 2970 and 2930 cm−1 [31]. The distinct peaks at 1663, 1637, 1560, 1400, and 1057 cm−1 correspond to C = O (amide), N–H, C = N, C–N, and C–O–C bonds, respectively [31,49–52]. Thus, the surface of n-CDs consists of hydrophilic groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2). To further verify this conclusion, we detected the XPS spectra of n-CDs. The XPS full spectra of n-CDs is shown in Fig. 2f, and the data indicates that the n-CDs are mainly composed of C (65%), O (10%), and N (25%). Fig. 2g shows the C 1 s spectra of n-CDs, which contain peaks at 284.6 (C–C/C = C), 285.9 (C–O/C–N), 287.7 (CONH), and 288.7 eV (C = O/C = N). The high-resolution XPS N 1 s spectra of the n-CDs consist of two peaks that correspond to C–N = C (pyridinic N) (399.4) and N–H (401.3 eV) (Fig. 2h). Moreover, C–N/C = N can facilitate RTP [39], as shown in Fig. 2i. There are two peaks in the high-resolution XPS O 1 s spectra, which correspond to C = O (531.6 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV) bonds, respectively.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption, fluorescence, and phosphorescent spectra were investigated to understand the optical properties of the n-CDs. The UV–Vis absorbance, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the n-CD solution are displayed in Fig. 3 a. These peaks at 230 ∼ 260 and 325 nm in the UV–Vis absorbance (red line), which are attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C and n-π* transitions of C = O/C = N, respectively [53]. In addition, the C = O/C = N groups can generate triplet excitons.The lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of n-CD solution are 8.95 ns and 11%, respectively (Figure S1–S2). For the n-CD powder, the fluorescence properties are similar to those of the n-CD solution (Figure S3). The maximum peak of the n-CD powder occurred at 501 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 418 nm. The inset of Fig. 3b shows that the n-CD powder exhibits blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm). Because of aggregation-induced quenching, the lifetime and fluorescence QY of the n-CD powder were lower than those of the n-CD solution; they were 5.25 ns and 9.8%, respectively (Figure S4–S5). As shown in Fig. 3c, the phosphorescence spectrum of the n-CD powder has a broad phosphorescence peak (380–750 nm) and its phosphorescence emission center is located at ∼ 534 nm. Figure S6 shows that the luminescent color of fluorescence and phosphorescence was marked in the chromaticity coordinates by calculating the fluorescence and phosphorescent spectra of n-CDs. Using the equation
τ
avg
= ∑
α i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
α i
τ
i
2
, the average lifetime of the n-CD powder was estimated as 139 ms (three lifetime components: 2.54 ms (8%), 22.98 ms (25%), and 198.08 ms (67%), under excitation at 365 nm). Based on these results, the energy transfer process of the n-CD luminescence is as follows, wherein the electrons are excited from the ground state to a high singlet excited state (S0 → Sn, 3.82 eV) when the n-CD powder is irradiated by a 365 nm UV light. Because of the radiative transition from the lowest singlet excited state (S1 → S0, 2.71 eV), the n-CDs exhibit blue fluorescence. For green phosphorescence, the electrons of the S1 state are transferred to the T1 state (triplet state) by the intersystem crossing process (S1 → T1, Δ Est = 0.39 eV). Moreover, the n-CDs possess several sub-fluorophores, and the H-bonds in n-CDs can be broken by the H2O molecules. Thus, the n-CD solution exhibits only fluorescence and no phosphorescence.

Subsequently, the phosphorescence stability of the carbon points was systematically studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when the n-CD powder was continuously irradiated by UV light (365 nm), the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CDs gradually decreased. The phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder was only reduced by 10% after 60 min of 365 nm UV light irradiation. Because the H-bonds can limit oxygen penetration, the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder is almost identical in air and in N2 atmosphere (Fig. 4b) [46]. Thus, the n-CD powder exhibited an excellent phosphorescence stability. In addition, the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder was stronger at 77 K than that at 289 K; the phosphorescence duration of the n-CD powder as seen by the naked eye at 77 K was longer than that at 289 K (Figure S7). This is because low temperature weakens the vibration and enhances long-lived triplet excitons. Therefore, we measured the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of the n-CD powder at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 4c, the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD powder decreases as the temperature increases from 20 to 70 °C. Therefore, the n-CD is expected to be used as a thermometer in ambient temperature measurements. Fig. 4d shows the phosphorescent digital photographs of the n-CD-based test paper at different temperatures (20–70 °C). The experimental data indicate that the phosphorescence intensity of the n-CD-based test paper varies linearly with temperature (y = -1267.39953x + 104648.07, R2 = 99.5) (Fig. 4e). Therefore, the as-prepared n-CD-based test paper as a probe can monitor temperature. Table S1 shows the experimental data obtained from temperature analysis using a thermometer and the n-CD-based test paper. The results indicate that the temperature determined by the n-CD-based test paper is consistent with that determined by the traditional thermometer, with a minor relative error. More importantly, the n-CD-based test paper can be used again after each temperature test (Figure S8). Therefore, based on the portability and accuracy of the n-CD-based test paper, we believe it has the potential to replace traditional thermometers.

The experimental data indicate that the n-CDs exhibit good water solubility and excellent room temperature phosphorescence properties. Therefore, the n-CDs can be used in monochrome printing as a marking ink. Fig. 5 a shows n-CD-based ink printed digital photographs with good resolution. The printed photographs on commercial parchment filter paper are only visible under UV light (365 nm) and exhibit blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the printed digital photographs exhibit green phosphorescence after turning off the UV light. Based on these results, an information encryption and decryption experiment was designed as follows: two types of CD-based inks (RTP: n-CD-based ink and No RTP: n-CDs-1-based ink) were used to print Chinaese characters on commercial parchment filter paper. When 365 nm UV light irradiated the printed paper, the characters printed by both inks were displayed (wrong information). However, after turning off the UV light, only the characters printed by the n-CD-based ink (correct information) emerged on the paper, exhibiting green phosphorescence. Thus, RTP n-CDs can be applied in the field of advanced information security as encryption inks.

In summary, matrix-free RTP n-CDs were successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. The cytotoxicity data showed that the as-prepared n-CDs had no bio-toxicity. The n-CDs not only exhibit a bright blue fluorescence, but also exhibit green RTP properties. The phosphorescence lifetime of n-CD powder is approximately 139 ms. Owing to the RTP property and good water solubility, n-CDs as encryption ink can be used in information encryption and decryption. More importantly, vibration quenches the long-lived triplet excitons. Thus, the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of the n-CD powder is affected by temperature, and an n-CD-based test paper can be used to effectively detect temperature. This study provides a novel strategy for the simple and rapid preparation of matrix-free RTP CD materials and expands the range of applications of RTP n-CDs.

Yujing Nie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Resources. Xuwei Chen: Data curation. Yuqin Wang: Data curation. Wenqiang Lai: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Nan Zheng: Data curation. Wen Weng: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (Nos. 2018H0030), the Education Bureau of Fujian Province of China (Nos. JZ160453), and the Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training of Students Platform (Nos. S202110402066). The funder had no role in study design, in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, in the writing of the report, and nor in the decision to submit the article for publication.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107126.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
